pattern: Process queries in sorted order, sweeping intervals into a min-heap keyed by size as their left endpoint comes into range and discarding intervals whose right endpoint has fallen behind, so the heap top is always the smallest interval covering the current query.
hints: Answering each query independently by scanning all intervals is too slow; sorting both intervals and queries lets you reuse work across queries.
Sort queries ascending and add every interval whose left endpoint is at or below the current query into a structure keyed by interval size.
Use a min-heap of (size, right); before reading an answer, pop intervals whose right endpoint is below the query, then the heap's smallest remaining size is the answer (or -1 if empty).

edge_inputs.description: single interval covers some queries and misses others
single query lands inside one interval
a wide interval contains a query but a narrower nested one is the real answer
queries given out of order must still pair with their own interval
an interval ending exactly at the query must still count

stress.description: random small-range intervals and queries to create many overlaps and gaps
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, size)
    intervals = []
    for _ in range(n):
        a = rng.randint(1, 200)
        b = rng.randint(a, 200)
        intervals.append([a, b])
    queries = [rng.randint(1, 220) for _ in range(n)]
    return [intervals, queries]
stress.note: dense overlaps stress heap eviction and tie handling

stress.description: wide-range sparse intervals so many queries miss every interval
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, size)
    intervals = []
    for _ in range(n):
        a = rng.randint(1, 10000000)
        b = rng.randint(a, min(10000000, a + rng.randint(0, 1000)))
        intervals.append([a, b])
    queries = [rng.randint(1, 10000000) for _ in range(n)]
    return [intervals, queries]
stress.note: sparse coverage exercises the -1 path frequently
